elements.sent-2411-001: Denoising with a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (D-JEPA), an autoregressive model, has demonstrated outstanding performance in class-conditional image generation.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the application of next-token prediction in high-resolution text-to-image generation remains underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we introduce D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I, an extension of D-JEPA incorporating flow matching loss, designed to enable data-efficient continuous resolution learning.
elements.sent-2411-004: D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I leverages a multimodal visual transformer to effectively integrate textual and visual features and adopts Visual Rotary Positional Embedding (VoPE) to facilitate continuous resolution learning.
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, we devise a data feedback mechanism that significantly enhances data utilization efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-006: For the first time, we achieve state-of-the-art high-resolution image synthesis via next-token prediction.
elements.sent-2411-007: The experimental code and pretrained models will be open-sourced at \url{https://d-jepa.github.io/t2i .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figure/teaser_.pdf} \caption{1K-resolution images sampled from D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I, showcasing its ability to generate high-fidelity, high-resolution images. (Best viewed when zoomed in.) D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I can accurately produce images at various aspect ratios, reaching up to 4K resolution. More examples can be found in the supplementary materials.} \label{fig: teaser}
elements.sent-2411-008: In recent years, diffusion models have become the standard method for generating high-resolution images and videos from natural language inputs, demonstrating remarkable generalization capabilities~\citep{saharia2022photorealistic, ramesh2022hierarchical, podell2023sdxl, dai2023emu, esser2023structure, blattmann2023align, betker2023improving, blattmann2023stable, singer2022makeavideo, esser2024scaling, song2019generative, song2020score, ho2020denoising, dhariwal2021diffusion, rombach2022high, song2020denoising, lipman2022flow, liu2022flow, karras2022elucidating, gao2024lumina, zhuo2024lumina .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Simultaneously, the advent of autoregressive large language models~\citep{radford2018improving, radford2019language, brown2020language, ouyang2022training, achiam2023gpt, anil2023palm, chowdhery2023palm, hoffmann2022training, touvron2023llama, touvron2023llama2, le2023bloom, sun2021ernie, bai2023qwen, yang2024qwen2, team2023gemini, raffel2020exploring, yang2019xlnet has ushered in a new era in artificial intelligence, marked by substantial strides towards general artificial intelligence due to their exceptional generality and versatility.}
elements.sent-2411-010: The transformative success of language models has also spurred advancements in image generation~\citep{ramesh2021zero, yu2021vector, yu2022scaling, sun2024autoregressive, liu2024lumina .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Recent studies, such as D-JEPA~\citep{chen2024denoising , MAR~\citep{li2024autoregressive , and VAR~\citep{tian2024visual , suggest that the generative capabilities of autoregressive models can rival or even surpass those of diffusion models in class-conditioned generation tasks on ImageNet~\citep{russakovsky2015imagenet .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Despite these achievements in natural language processing, autoregressive models still fall short in computer vision, particularly in high-resolution text-to-image synthesis~\citep{fan2024fluid,liu2024customize , compared to diffusion models.}
elements.sent-2411-013: As noted by~\citeauthor{kilian2024computational , current autoregressive models underperform in generating high-resolution images, specifically in terms of image texture and overall quality, although they exhibit superior prompt following and throughput.}
elements.sent-2411-014: To improve autoregressive models for image generation and foster the development of unified multi-modal models, we focus on two primary aspects: model architecture and data utilization.
elements.sent-2411-015: From a model architecture perspective, several issues need resolution.
elements.sent-2411-016: First, the potential of representational learning to enhance model performance has been largely overlooked, as indicated in~\citep{chen2024denoising, yu2024representation .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Second, autoregressive models have been isolated from other generative models, notably diffusion models, hindering the adoption of successful methods from diverse domains~\citep{li2024autoregressive .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Third, the patchify operation, which divides continuous images into discrete blocks identified via RoPE~\citep{su2024roformer , impedes the modeling of continuous resolutions and aspect ratios.}
elements.sent-2411-019: To address these challenges, we devise D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I, which employs a multimodal visual transformer block and introduces a more adaptable flow matching loss.
elements.sent-2411-020: Additionally, we propose visual rotary positional embedding (VoPE) to enable continuous-resolution and dynamic-aspect learning.
elements.sent-2411-021: Regarding data utilization, we introduce a data feedback mechanism to leverage resources more effectively, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig: data-feedback .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Traditional data curation focuses on preprocessing tasks, such as filtering low-quality images~\citep{he2022rethinking and optimizing prompts using multimodal models~\citep{li2024hunyuan .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Although these methods enhance data quality, they often introduce inherent biases and fail to address evolving data distributions during training, especially with billion-scale datasets.
elements.sent-2411-024: Model fine-tuning strategies like reinforcement learning from human feedback~\citep{xu2024imagereward, liang2024rich and direct preference optimization~\citep{rafailov2024direct, wallace2024diffusion act as remedial measures with inconsistent effectiveness.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: In contrast, our data feedback mechanism continuously adjusts the data distribution based on real-time statistical analysis and model performance during training, ensuring optimal data utilization and enhancing the reliability and effectiveness of the training process.
elements.sent-2411-026: By integrating data feedback in the training of D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I, we achieve state-of-the-art high-resolution image synthesis through next-token prediction, as validated on the T2I-CompBench~\citep{huang2023t2i , GenEval~\citep{ghosh2024geneval and GenAI-Bench~\citep{li2024genai benchmarks, as well as human ratings.}}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{subfigure}{0.5\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figure/djepa_t2i_.pdf} \caption{Overview of all components.} \label{fig: framework} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.5\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figure/mmvit.pdf} \caption{Multi-modal visual transformer block.} \label{fig: mmvit} \end{subfigure} \caption{Denoising with a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for text-to-image synthesis. We present the overview of D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I in Fig.~\ref{fig: framework}. We employ T5-XXL~\citep{chung2024scaling} as the text encoder, and the KL-VAE pretrained by \citet{esser2024scaling} as the image encoder. Both textual and visual tokens are trimmed to no more than 256 and $256^2$ tokens for efficient training, respectively. The feature predictor $\gamma$, the context encoder $\phi$, and the target encoder $\bar{\phi}$ share the same network architecture, each consisting of several multimodal visual transformer blocks, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig: mmvit}. The gradient is detached from the output of the target encoder $\bar{\phi}$, ensuring that it is only updated via Exponential Moving Average (EMA). Both the prediction loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{pred}}$ and the flow matching loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{flow}}$ are computed only for the masked visual tokens, following \citet{chen2024denoising}.} \label{fig: djepa t2i}
elements.sent-2411-027: D-JEPA, originally proposed by \citet{chen2024denoising , is based on a visual transformer structure~\citep{dosovitskiy2020image that models the token distribution $p(x_i|z_i)$ using feature prediction loss $\mathcal{L _{\text{pred }$ and diffusion loss $\mathcal{L _{\text{diff }$, where $z_i$ represents the predicted features of each token, respectively.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: D-JEPA has demonstrated significant advantages in class-conditioned image generation on ImageNet.
elements.sent-2411-029: However, the original D-JEPA architecture is limited to generating fixed-scale resolution images (typically $256 \times 256$ or $512 \times 512$) conditioned solely on class labels.
elements.sent-2411-030: In this work, we introduce D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I to extend its capabilities to high-resolution text-to-image synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-031: To achieve this, we have adapted a multimodal visual transformer (Fig.~\ref{fig: mmvit ) that more effectively integrates textual and visual features.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Additionally, we replace the diffusion loss $\mathcal{L _{\text{diff }$ used in D-JEPA with a more flexible and faster-converging flow matching loss $\mathcal{L _{\text{flow }$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: These enhancements enable D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I to perform more effectively in high-resolution text-to-image synthesis, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig: djepa t2i .}
elements.sent-2411-034: The multimodal visual transformer draws inspiration from the design of the multimodal diffusion backbone, initially proposed by \citet{esser2024scaling .}
elements.sent-2411-035: The core idea is that text and image embeddings are conceptually quite different, necessitating the use of two separate sets of weights for the two modalities.
elements.sent-2411-036: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig: mmvit , this approach is equivalent to having two independent transformers for each modality but joining the sequences of the two modalities for the attention operation, allowing both representations to operate within their own spaces while still accounting for the other.}
elements.sent-2411-037: To avoid this, they propose normalizing Q and K before the attention operation.
elements.sent-2411-038: We follow this approach and use RMSNorm~\citep{zhang2019root with a learnable scale in both streams of our D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I architecture, similar to~\citet{esser2024scaling .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: The additional normalization prevents attention logit growth instability, confirming findings by previous works\citep{dehghani2023scaling, wortsman2023smallscale, esser2024scaling , and enables efficient training at bf16-mixed precision~\citep{chen2019bfloat16 when combined with the AdamW~\citep{loshchilov2017fixing optimizer.}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The primary distinction between the multimodal visual transformer and the multimodal diffusion backbone is that the former does not require handling the additional timestep $t$ introduced by the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-041: This omission sidesteps adaptive layer norm~\citep{perez2018film , which is essential for diffusion models built on top of DiT~\citep{peebles2023scalable .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: For textual tokens, we follow the design principles of large language models and use RoPE~\citep{su2024roformer as positional encoding.}
elements.sent-2411-043: For visual tokens, we introduce VoPE, a positional encoding specifically designed for visual features, which we detail in Para.~\ref{para:vope .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Flow models~\citep{ma2024sit, mo2024freecontrol, lipman2022flow are generally used for generating a complete image.}
elements.sent-2411-045: In this context, we adhere to the flow matching formulation presented in \citet{gao2024lumina for modeling the token distribution $p(x_i|z_i)$.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Flow matching~\citep{lipman2022flow, albergo2023stochastic emerges as a simple alternative that linearly interpolates between noise and data along a straight line.}
elements.sent-2411-047: More specifically, given the data \( x_i \sim p(x_i|z_i) \) and Gaussian noise \( \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N (0, I) \), we define an interpolation-based forward process: \begin{equation}x_i^t = \alpha_t x_i + \beta_t \epsilon,\end{equation} where \( \alpha_0 = 0 \), \( \beta_t = 1 \), \( \alpha_1 = 1 \), and \( \beta_1 = 0 \).}
elements.sent-2411-048: This interpolation for \( t \in [0,1] \) bridges \( x_i^0 = \epsilon \) and \( x_i^1 = x_i \).
elements.sent-2411-049: Similar to the diffusion schedule, this interpolation schedule offers flexible choices of \( \alpha_t \) and \( \beta_t \).
elements.sent-2411-050: In our framework, we adopt a linear interpolation schedule between noise and data for its simplicity: $x_i^t = t x_i + (1-t) \epsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-051: This formulation represents a uniform transformation with constant velocity between the data and noise.
elements.sent-2411-052: The corresponding time-dependent velocity field is defined as: \begin{equation}v_t(x_i^t, z_i) = \dot{\alpha}_t x_i + \dot{\beta}_t \epsilon = x_i - \epsilon,\end{equation} where \( \dot{\alpha \) and \( \dot{\beta \) denote the time derivatives of \( \alpha \) and \( \beta \).}}
elements.sent-2411-053: This time-dependent velocity field \( v : [0,1] \times \mathbb{R ^d \to \mathbb{R ^d \) defines an ordinary differential equation known as the Flow ODE: \begin{equation}dx_i = v_t(x_i^t, z_i) dt.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: We use \( \psi_t(x_i, z_i) \) to represent the solution of the Flow ODE with the initial condition \( \psi_0(x_i, z_i) = x_i \).
elements.sent-2411-055: By solving this Flow ODE from \( t = 0 \) to \( t = 1 \), we transform noise into data samples using the approximated velocity fields \( v_{\theta (x_i^t, t, z_i) \).}
elements.sent-2411-056: Similar to the approaches of \citet{chen2024denoising and \citet{li2024autoregressive , $v_{\theta}$ is implemented with a small denoising MLP~\citep{li2024autoregressive .}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: During training, the flow matching objective directly regresses to the target velocity for each token: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{flow}}(x_i, z_i) = \int_0^1 \mathbb{E}\left[ \parallel v_{\theta}(x_i^t, t, z_i) - (x_i - \epsilon) \parallel^2 \right] dt, \label{eq: flow matching loss}\end{equation} which is termed the conditional flow matching loss, sharing similarities with the noise prediction or score prediction losses in diffusion models~\citep{lipman2022flow .}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}L_{flow}(x_i, z_i) = \int_0^1 E\left[ \parallel v_{\theta}(x_i^t, t, z_i) - (x_i - \epsilon) \parallel^2 \right] dt, \label{eq: flow matching loss}\end{equation}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require $T$: Number of auto-regressive steps, $N$: Total tokens to sample. \State Initialize: $\mathbb{X} \gets \emptyset$ \For{$n$ in $\text{cosine-step-function}(T, N)$} \State $\mathbb{C} \gets \phi(\mathbb{X})$ \Comment{Encode the sampled tokens} \State $\mathbb{Z} \gets \gamma(\mathbb{C})$ \Comment{Predict features of unsampled tokens} \State $\{z_0, \ldots, z_n\} \sim \mathbb{Z}$ \Comment{Randomly select $n$ tokens} \State $\{x_0, \ldots, x_n\} \gets \text{denoise}(\epsilon_\theta, \{z_0, \ldots, z_n\})$ \State $\mathbb{X} \gets \mathbb{X} \cup \{x_0, \ldots, x_n\}$ \Comment{Add the denoised tokens} \EndFor \State Return: $\mathbb{X}$ \end{algorithmic} \caption{High-resolution image synthesis with D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I in generalized next-token prediction.} \label{alg: sampling}
elements.sent-2411-058: For evaluating generative models in generalized next-token prediction, we employ an iterative sampling strategy similar to those used in \citet{chang2022maskgit, li2024autoregressive , as outlined in Algo.~\ref{alg: sampling .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: This strategy gradually decreases the masking ratio from \(1.0\) to \(0.0\) following a cosine schedule, typically using \(64\) autoregressive steps for sampling an image with a resolution of $256\times256$.
elements.sent-2411-060: Empirically, we find that even for higher-resolution images (such as 2K or 4K), satisfactory sampling quality can be achieved within approximately 100 autoregressive steps.
elements.sent-2411-061: D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I follows the approach of \citet{chen2024denoising , utilizing fully randomized orderings to determine the next set of tokens to predict.}
elements.sent-2411-062: This design effectively enhances the diversity of the generated samples.
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figure/rope_decay.pdf} \caption{RoPE.} \label{fig: rope decay} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figure/vope_decay.pdf} \caption{VoPE.} \label{fig: vope decay} \end{subfigure} \caption{Comparison of decay curves between RoPE and VoPE.} \label{fig: vope}
elements.sent-2411-063: Diffusion models learn the distribution of entire images, while autoregressive models focus on learning the distribution for each masked token.
elements.sent-2411-064: Empirically, autoregressive models face challenges when generating images with arbitrary resolutions and aspect ratios, mainly due to the absence of appropriate visual positional embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-065: Both sinusoidal positional encoding and rotary positional embedding have limitations: the former cannot ensure positional consistency when images are cropped or padded, and neither can maintain positional information consistency across different scales of the same image.
elements.sent-2411-066: Consequently, operations such as cropping, padding, or scaling an image can lead to models receiving completely different positional information if existing positional embedding schemes are applied directly.
elements.sent-2411-067: These limitations significantly impact the learning of token distributions, potentially leading to flaws in generated high-resolution images.
elements.sent-2411-068: Visual-rotary Positional Embedding (VoPE) is inspired by biological vision and camera imaging principles.
elements.sent-2411-069: VoPE assumes that all objects are projected within a field of view with a fixed resolution of $g \times g$.
elements.sent-2411-070: For an image with resolution $W \times H$, any pixel at coordinate $(w, h)$ can be normalized as: \begin{equation}\left\{  (\frac{1}{\rho}(w+b), \frac{1}{\rho}(h)), W \le H (\frac{1}{\rho}(w), \frac{1}{\rho}(h+b)), W > H  \right.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-071: This normalization centers images on the grid \(g \times g\), facilitating efficient learning from images of varying resolutions.
elements.sent-2411-072: Consider a single row of tokens where \( q_m \) and \( k_n \) are tokens from query and key sequences, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-073: VoPE targets the dot product between \( q_m \) and \( k_n \) to incorporate the relative positional information \(\frac{1 {\rho (m-n)\) while maintaining translation invariance.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Thus, the inner product encodes positional information in a relative pixel normalized manner: \begin{equation}\langle f_q(x_m, \frac{1}{\rho}(m+b)), f_k(x_n, \frac{1}{\rho}(n + b)) \rangle = g(x_m, x_n, \frac{1}{\rho}(m-n)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-075: The aim is to determine an equivalent encoding mechanism for \( f_q(x_m, \frac{1 {\rho (m+b)) \) and \( f_k(x_n, \frac{1 {\rho (n+b)) \) that satisfies the above relation.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Referring to \citet{su2024roformer , the functions \( f \) and \( g \) that meet the relationship can be defined as follows when the feature dimension \( d = 2 \): \begin{equation}f_q(x_m, \frac{1}{\rho}(m + b)) = (W_q x_m)e^{i [\frac{1}{\rho}(m + b)] \theta} f_k(x_n, \frac{1}{\rho}(n + b)) = (W_k x_n)e^{i [\frac{1}{\rho}(n+b)] \theta} g(x_m, x_n, \frac{1}{\rho}(m-n)) = Re[(W_q x_m)(W_k x_n)^{\star}e^{i[\frac{1}{\rho}(m-n)]\theta}] ,\end{equation} where \( \text{Re [\cdot] \) denotes the real part, and \( (\mathbf{W _k x_n)^\star \) is the complex conjugate of \( (\mathbf{W _k x_n) \).}}}}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}f_q(x_m, \frac{1}{\rho}(m + b)) = (W_q x_m)e^{i [\frac{1}{\rho}(m + b)] \theta} f_k(x_n, \frac{1}{\rho}(n + b)) = (W_k x_n)e^{i [\frac{1}{\rho}(n+b)] \theta} g(x_m, x_n, \frac{1}{\rho}(m-n)) = Re[(W_q x_m)(W_k x_n)^{\star}e^{i[\frac{1}{\rho}(m-n)]\theta}] ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-078: When \( d > 2 \), \( \theta_j = \omega^{-\frac{2(j-1) {d , j \in [1, 2, \dots, d/2] \), where \( \omega \) is a preset base frequency.}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: For larger \( d \), the derivations and expressions of \( f_q(x_m, \frac{1 {\rho (m + b)) \) and \( f_k(x_n, \frac{1 {\rho (n + b)) \) are consistent with RoPE~\citep{su2024roformer and are not reiterated here.}}}}}

Here, $p$ represents the portion of prompts containing this notion.} \label{fig: dist}
elements.sent-2411-080: During sampling, RoPE requires adjusting the base frequency $\omega$ for higher resolution images.
elements.sent-2411-081: For instance, with a 256 token training length and a target of 512 tokens, the NTK-Aware Scaled RoPE approach~\citep{peng2023ntk is used, where $\omega^{\prime = \frac{\omega {2}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Although effective for long texts, this causes discrepancies in positional information between training and sampling phases, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig: rope decay .}
elements.sent-2411-083: These discrepancies are detrimental for image generation, which is sensitive to token boundary information.
elements.sent-2411-084: Thus, RoPE is unsuitable for arbitrary resolution image generation, especially at higher resolutions.
elements.sent-2411-085: In contrast, VoPE ensures that pixel normalization maintains consistent positional information across resolutions during training and sampling.
elements.sent-2411-086: This is achieved by normalizing images to a $g \times g$ grid via \(\rho\), without changing the base frequency \(\omega\), regardless of resolution.
elements.sent-2411-087: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig: vope decay , images at different resolutions with VoPE use the same relative positional curve at varying resolution densities \(\rho\).}
elements.sent-2411-088: Notably, at \(\rho = 1/4\), the curve closely matches that of \(\rho = 1.0\), implying that to generate $4096 \times 4096$ images, training at $1024 \times 1024$ with \(\rho = 1/4\) suffices.\footnote{This assumes a VAE encoding stride of 8 and a patch size of 2.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Beyond enabling different resolution image generation via \(\rho\), VoPE allows layout adjustments in generated images with varying aspect ratios by adjusting the relative position bias \(b\).

elements.subsection-2411-003: Data Feedback for Efficient Data Utilization
elements.sent-2411-091: While \citet{esser2024scaling and \citet{li2024hunyuan meticulously curate high-quality training data, they lack an in-depth examination of the actual sampled training data.}}
elements.sent-2411-092: It is worth noting that the curated training data does not always match the sampled training data and does not guarantee an optimal text-to-image model due to inherent data biases in natural distributions and the potential loss of long-tail data due to sampling.
elements.sent-2411-093: The distribution of training data obtained through curation rules typically presents significant biases.
elements.sent-2411-094: As illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig: dist , since a large volume of training data is often sourced from publicly available internet data, the overall distribution, even post-curation, shows strong concentration regarding resolution and semantic notions.}
elements.sent-2411-095: This can result in redundant data, thereby reducing the model's overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-096: Directly training with vast datasets (usually ranging from tens of millions to billions of image-text pairs) can potentially lead to the loss of long-tail data.
elements.sent-2411-097: In small-scale training, such as with ImageNet, the entire dataset is extensively traversed (e.g., thousands of iterations in \citet{peebles2023scalable ), providing the model ample opportunity to learn the distribution of all training data.}
elements.sent-2411-098: However, when the dataset scales up to billions, each data point might only be traversed a few times, and often the entire dataset might not be fully traversed even once.
elements.sent-2411-099: Resuming training typically recovers prior model parameters and optimizer states but struggles to track previously trained data indices, exacerbating the loss of long-tail data.
elements.sent-2411-100: To address these challenges, we propose a data feedback mechanism that dynamically adjusts the data distribution during training.
elements.sent-2411-101: The training pipeline is depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig: data-feedback .}
elements.sent-2411-102: We perform statistical analysis on each batch of sampled data, examining features such as resolution, prompt composition, and style tags, and feed these features back into the sampling process for subsequent iterations in real-time.
elements.sent-2411-103: Furthermore, we conduct comprehensive performance evaluations on saved model checkpoints to promptly adjust subsequent data sampling strategies.
elements.sent-2411-104: When sampling data, we introduce two distinct strategies to handle transformable and non-transformable attributes.
elements.sent-2411-105: For transformable attributes such as image resolution and aspect ratio, which can be adjusted without quality degradation, we utilize a truncated normal distribution sampling strategy, denoted as $\mathrm{trunc\_norm (\mu,\sigma,a,b)$.}
elements.sent-2411-106: We initially sample the desired data parameters, such as the target resolution.
elements.sent-2411-107: If the current data can be transformed to align with these specified parameters, we proceed with the transformed data; otherwise, we discard this sample and attempt resampling.
elements.sent-2411-108: In contrast, for non-transformable attributes, we decide whether to use the current data based on a predetermined sampling frequency specific to each attribute.
elements.sent-2411-109: If the data exceeds this frequency, there is a 50\% probability it will be discarded.
elements.sent-2411-110: Although this approach may not strictly adhere to the exact predetermined sampling frequencies, it significantly enhances data utilization, particularly beneficial when dealing with the sampling of long-tail distributions.

elements.sent-2411-111: In this section, we succinctly outline the experimental configuration and training process of D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I.
elements.sent-2411-112: We evaluate the model performance through automated metrics, qualitative results, and human ratings.
elements.sent-2411-113: Detailed experimental setups and crucial ablation studies (concerning VoPE, data feedback, and more) are provided in the supplementary material.
elements.sent-2411-114: We employ an internally curated dataset comprising over 1 billion image-text pairs for training.
elements.sent-2411-115: Each image has a minimum shorter side length of 512 pixels.
elements.sent-2411-116: To maintain high quality, images with aesthetic scores below $5.0$ are excluded using the LAION-AI aesthetic predictor\footnote{\url{github.com/LAION-AI/aesthetic-predictor .}}
elements.sent-2411-117: Additionally, OCR tools filter out images containing text, which constrains the model from generating text but enhances its learning of other real-world concepts.
elements.sent-2411-118: English captions are generated for each image using InternVL2~\citep{chen2023internvl, chen2024far , and enhanced with image tags (e.g., style tags, data source).}
elements.sent-2411-119: Synthetic datasets, like JourneyDB~\citep{sun2024journeydb , are carefully incorporated despite their efficacy in accelerating convergence~\citep{chen2023pixart, gao2024lumina .}}
elements.sent-2411-120: We hypothesize that over-dependence on synthetic data might limit the model's output diversity, reducing data utility.
elements.sent-2411-121: Thus, synthetic data constitutes only about 5\% of the dataset, ensuring D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I generates authentic content, albeit with slower convergence.

elements.sent-2411-122: The D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I model is an extension of D-JEPA-H~\citep{chen2024denoising with 2.6 billion parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-123: Training is strategically split into two phases for optimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-124: Initially, the model is trained on fixed-resolution images of $256 \times 256$ pixels using a batch size of 2048 across 1 million steps.
elements.sent-2411-125: This phase is crucial for enhancing textual concept comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-126: A cosine annealing schedule adjusts the learning rate from $1 \times 10^{-5}$ to $1 \times 10^{-6}$, with a warm-up of 100k steps.
elements.sent-2411-127: The second phase diversifies image scales and resolutions with batches dynamically based on sampled resolutions from a truncated normal distribution (see Sec.~\ref{sec: data feedback ).}
elements.sent-2411-129: Image resolutions progress from approximately $128$ to $384$ pixels, reaching up to 1024 pixels eventually.
elements.sent-2411-130: Batch sizes are adjusted according to image size, maintaining a constant learning rate of $5 \times 10^{-5}$ over an additional 200k steps.
elements.sent-2411-131: Evaluations occur every 40 epochs, measured by ImageNet~\citep{russakovsky2015imagenet , to refine data sampling strategies via T2I benchmark metrics and human feedback.}
elements.sent-2411-132: The training is executed on 128 H800 GPUs over a month.
elements.sent-2411-134: The D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I model can generate images at arbitrary resolutions and aspect ratios.
elements.sent-2411-135: For $256 \times 256$ resolution images used in quantitative evaluation, we set autoregressive steps to $T=64$.
elements.sent-2411-136: For higher resolutions, autoregressive steps are empirically tuned.
elements.sent-2411-137: Classifier-free guidance~\cite{ho2021classifier enhances image quality, with hyperparameters optimized per benchmark.}
elements.sent-2411-138: Denosing MLP diffusion steps are consistently set to $250$ across tasks.

elements.sent-2411-294: Recently, autoregressive models have demonstrated remarkable performance in class-conditional image generation.
elements.sent-2411-295: However, the application of next-token prediction to high-resolution text-to-image generation remains largely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-296: In this paper, we introduce D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I, an autoregressive model based on continuous tokens that incorporates innovations in both architecture and training strategy to generate high-quality, photorealistic images at arbitrary resolutions, up to 4K.
elements.sent-2411-297: Architecturally, we adopt the denoising joint embedding predictive architecture (D-JEPA) while leveraging a multimodal visual transformer to effectively integrate textual and visual features.
elements.sent-2411-298: Additionally, we introduce flow matching loss alongside the proposed Visual Rotary Positional Embedding (VoPE) to enable continuous resolution learning.
elements.sent-2411-299: In terms of training strategy, we propose a data feedback mechanism that dynamically adjusts the sampling procedure based on statistical analysis and an online learning critic model.

elements.sent-2411-302: At the same time, the rise of autoregressive large language models~\citep{radford2018improving, radford2019language, brown2020language, ouyang2022training, achiam2023gpt, anil2023palm, chowdhery2023palm, hoffmann2022training, touvron2023llama, touvron2023llama2, le2023bloom, sun2021ernie, bai2023qwen, yang2024qwen2, team2023gemini, raffel2020exploring, yang2019xlnet has marked a new era in artificial intelligence, leading to significant advances in artificial general intelligence~(AGI) due to their unparalleled versatility and generality.}

elements.sent-2411-333: Visual-rotary positional embedding (VoPE) is inspired by biological vision and camera imaging principles.
elements.sent-2411-334: To address this, we propose a statistical analysis and critic model sampling approach for more refined data selection.
elements.sent-2411-335: We refer to this novel training strategy as data feedback, and the training pipeline is depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig: data-feedback .}
elements.sent-2411-336: Since a large volume of training data is often sourced from publicly available internet data, the overall distribution, even post-curation, shows strong concentration regarding resolution and semantic notions.~\footnote{Please This can result in redundant data, thereby reducing the model's overall performance.}
elements.sent-2411-337: To address these challenges, we perform statistical analysis on each batch of sampled data, examining features such as resolution, prompt composition, and style tags, and feed these features back into the sampling process for subsequent iterations in real-time.
elements.sent-2411-338: Based on statistical analysis sampling, we can achieve a more reliable and balanced selection, ensuring uniform sampling of all data types as expected.
elements.sent-2411-339: Does uniformly sampling all types of data necessarily lead to a well-trained generative model? The answer is clearly No. While diverse data distribution ensures a broad representation within the dataset, diversity alone is merely a fundamental prerequisite for training a model with general capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-340: Critic model sampling is designed to address the inherent weaknesses of the model during the training process.
elements.sent-2411-341: In the early training stages, we employ statistical analysis sampling to expose the model to a wide variety of data, helping it establish a foundational knowledge system.
elements.sent-2411-342: However, in practice, we observe that the model quickly learns to generate certain types of content while struggling with others.
elements.sent-2411-343: Some types of data may take significantly longer to learn, or in some cases, the model may never master them.
elements.sent-2411-344: Critic model sampling aims to identify the types of content that the model finds challenging and prioritize them during training.
elements.sent-2411-345: By doing so, we continuously refine the model's abilities and mitigate its weaknesses.
elements.figure-2411-028: [] \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figure/critic_model_img.pdf} \caption{The pipeline to prepare training set for critic model.} \label{fig: critic model}
elements.sent-2411-346: A key aspect of this approach involves the online learning of the critic model.
elements.sent-2411-347: As illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig: critic model , we follow a structured pipeline for training the critic model.. (a) Data preparation.}
elements.sent-2411-348: First, we collect a sufficiently large set of prompts that cover a comprehensive range of scenarios (typically in the thousands).
elements.sent-2411-349: Next, we load the current T2I model weights and generate a substantial number of synthetic images for each prompt to form a reference set.
elements.sent-2411-350: Additionally, we retrieve several images from the training dataset based on text similarity with each prompt to construct the training set.. (b) Data annotation.
elements.sent-2411-351: For the reference set, in the early training stages—when the model's performance is still suboptimal—we utilize automated evaluation metrics such as T2I CompBench~\citep{huang2023t2i to assess whether generated images meet the fundamental requirements of the prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-352: Images that meet the criteria are labeled as positive samples, while those that fail are labeled as negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-353: As training progresses and the model's output quality improves, we introduce human evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-354: At this stage, experts assess each generated image from a professional photography perspective, considering aspects such as logical consistency and realism.
elements.sent-2411-355: Images that meet the quality criteria are labeled as positive samples, while those that do not are marked as negative.
elements.sent-2411-356: For the training set, after completing the reference set annotations, we compute the feature similarity between images in the training set and their corresponding reference set images.
elements.sent-2411-357: Images with high feature similarity inherit the reference set labels, while those with low similarity are discarded.
elements.sent-2411-358: Since the model is still relatively weak in the early stages, we adopt a lower similarity threshold for automatically labeled reference images.
elements.sent-2411-359: In later stages, when human annotations are available, we raise the threshold to ensure a sufficiently large and high-quality training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-360: We employ a ResNet-18 model~\citep{he2016deep , modifying it into a binary classification network.}
elements.sent-2411-361: For the first round of critic model training, we initialize it with ImageNet pre-trained weights collected by \citeauthor{rw2019timm .}
elements.sent-2411-362: In subsequent updates, we use the previous version of the critic model as the initialization.
elements.sent-2411-363: Since the T2I model continuously evolves, the critic model must also be updated accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-364: In our implementation, we introduce critic model sampling after the first 100K iterations of the T2I model.
elements.sent-2411-365: Thereafter, we update the critic model every 40K iterations.
elements.sent-2411-366: The training process follows standard ImageNet classification strategies and data augmentation techniques~\citep{rw2019timm .}
elements.sent-2411-367: Given that the dataset consists of tens of thousands of samples, the computational overhead of training the critic model is negligible.
elements.sent-2411-368: Once training is complete, we validate the samples obtained from statistical analysis sampling.
elements.sent-2411-369: The critic model assigns each sample a probability of being rejected (i.e., the likelihood that the model struggles with this type of content).
elements.sent-2411-370: Since we aim to sample more challenging cases where the model performs poorly, a higher rejection probability indicates that the model handles the sample well and should be discarded.
elements.sent-2411-371: Conversely, lower-probability samples are prioritized for training.
elements.paragraph-2411-031: Comparison between critic model sampling and fine-tuning techniques.
elements.sent-2411-372: Fine-tuning techniques, such as reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF)~\citep{xu2024imagereward, liang2024rich and direct preference optimization (DPO)~\citep{rafailov2024direct, wallace2024diffusion , provide post-hoc adjustments but often exhibit inconsistent effectiveness.}}
elements.sent-2411-373: Our proposed critic model sampling can be seen as an online counterpart to fine-tuning techniques.
elements.sent-2411-374: Moreover, the utilization of human annotation data differs between these approaches.
elements.sent-2411-375: In the fine-tuning stage, human annotators typically collect a new source of high-quality training data to directly improve the T2I model.
elements.sent-2411-376: In contrast, critic model sampling requires only a few thousand synthetic images to be annotated with positive and negative labels, which are then used solely for training the critic model—without necessitating additional new training data sources.
elements.sent-2411-377: In summary, critic model sampling focuses on improving the utilization of existing data, whereas fine-tuning techniques emphasize incorporating new data sources to enhance model performance.

elements.sent-2411-378: The D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I model extends D-JEPA-H~\citep{chen2024denoising and comprises 2.6 billion parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-379: Training is strategically divided into two phases.
elements.sent-2411-380: Initially, the model is trained on images no larger than $256 \times 256$ pixels\footnote{For images with resolutions greater than $256 \times 256$, we apply aspect ratio-preserving resizing.}
elements.sent-2411-381: A cosine annealing schedule is employed, adjusting the learning rate from $1 \times 10^{-5}$ to $1 \times 10^{-6}$, with a warm-up of 10k steps.
elements.sent-2411-382: In the second phase, the model trains on diverse image scales and resolutions, with batch sizes dynamically adjusted based on sampled resolutions from a truncated normal distribution (see Para.~\ref{para: sas ).}
elements.sent-2411-383: Image resolutions progressively increase from $128$ to $1024$ pixels over the subsequent 500k iterations.
elements.sent-2411-384: Refer to the supplementary material for more details on resolution sampling.
elements.sent-2411-385: During this phase, critic model sampling is introduced and updated every 40k iterations.
elements.sent-2411-386: Empirically, we find that with statistical analysis sampling alone, the model achieves an overall score of $0.50$ on GenEval within 100k training steps, matching the performance of SD2.0~\citep{rombach2022high and PixArt-$\alpha$~\citep{chen2023pixart .}}
elements.sent-2411-387: After introducing the critic model, trained with the automated evaluation-based trick, the model approaches its final performance at 200k steps, showing gradual improvement between 200k and 300k steps, ultimately reaching $0.66$—surpassing models of comparable scale.
elements.sent-2411-388: In the final 300k training steps, the reference set is labeled via human evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-389: While this stage does not significantly improve performance on standard benchmarks like GenEval~\citep{ghosh2024geneval , it leads to substantial advancements in image realism and photographic style, as reflected in human ratings, which increased from a win rate of less than 20\% against Midjourney v6 to over 30\%.}
elements.sent-2411-390: The full training process was conducted on 128 H800 GPUs over two weeks.
elements.sent-2411-391: The reference set required for updating the critic model was constructed using two different approaches based on the annotation method.
elements.sent-2411-392: During the automated annotation phase (100k–300k steps), we generated approximately 100k images at $256 \times 256$ resolution, as this stage primarily targets fundamental model capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-393: Since automated annotation mainly detects the presence of corresponding concepts, generating low-resolution images not only reduces inference time but also ensures comprehensive model evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-394: In the later stage (300k–600k steps), we generated around 10k images at resolutions ranging from $512$ to $1024$ pixels.
elements.sent-2411-395: At this point, the model had already acquired strong generative abilities, allowing human annotators to conduct meticulous evaluations and enforce stricter selection criteria, further driving model optimization.
elements.sent-2411-396: Before conducting the final evaluation on human rating, we performed direct preference optimization~(DPO) training.
elements.sent-2411-397: This training utilized an additional ultra-high-quality dataset and was conducted for 20k steps.
elements.sent-2411-398: The primary objective of this stage was to refine the model's image style and texture, making them more closely resemble real photographic aesthetics.
elements.sent-2411-399: Notably, we did not attempt to enhance other aspects of the model, such as text generation quality.
elements.sent-2411-400: We evaluate our method using automated metrics on prominent text-to-image benchmarks, including GenEval~\citep{ghosh2024geneval , T2I-CompBench~\citep{huang2023t2i , and GenAI-Bench~\citep{li2024genai , which assess the model's capacity to generate prompt-reflective images.}}}
elements.sent-2411-401: Table~\ref{tab: geneval compbench short compares D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I against state-of-the-art diffusion and autoregressive models, both open-source and closed-source.}
elements.sent-2411-402: Per GenEval overall scores in Tab.~\ref{tab: geneval compbench short , D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I surpasses other models within small and mainstream sizes.}
elements.sent-2411-403: Fig.~\ref{fig: new teaser illustrates the versatile capabilities of images generated by D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I, which supports flexible resolutions and aspect ratios and can adeptly handle various styles.}
elements.sent-2411-404: In this work, we introduce D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I, along with the VoPE positional encoding and a data feedback training strategy, aimed at enhancing the capability of autoregressive models to generate high-resolution images.
elements.sent-2411-405: Our empirical results demonstrate comprehensive outperformance over both diffusion models and existing autoregressive models in the text-to-image generation task.
elements.sent-2411-406: Future research will delve into further validation of scaling laws on D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I and investigate the effective application of the D-JEPA architecture to video generation, as well as building a unified multimodal model.. \setcounter{page {1 supplementary.}}

elements.figure-2411-033: \begin{subfigure}{0.5\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figure/djepa_t2i_img.pdf} \caption{Overview of all components.} \label{fig: framework} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.5\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figure/mmvit.pdf} \caption{Multi-modal visual transformer block.} \label{fig: mmvit} \end{subfigure} \caption{Denoising with a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for text-to-image synthesis. We present the overview of D-JEPA$\cdot$T2I in Fig.~\ref{fig: framework}. We employ T5-XXL~\citep{chung2024scaling} as the text encoder, and the KL-VAE pretrained by \citet{esser2024scaling} as the image encoder. Both textual and visual tokens are trimmed to no more than 256 and $256^2$ tokens for efficient training, respectively. The feature predictor $\gamma$, the context encoder $\phi$, and the target encoder $\bar{\phi}$ share the same network architecture, each consisting of several multimodal visual transformer blocks, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig: mmvit}. The gradient is detached from the output of the target encoder $\bar{\phi}$, ensuring that it is only updated via Exponential Moving Average (EMA). Both the prediction loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{pred}}$ and the flow matching loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{flow}}$ are computed only for the masked visual tokens, following \citet{chen2024denoising}.} \label{fig: djepa t2i}
